quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ideal gas law"?

options.text: d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
PV = nRT; R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); relates macroscopic state variables; valid when gas molecules are far apart and non-interacting
Intensity I ∝ A²; for spherical waves: I = P/(4πr²), intensity decreases as 1/r²
P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ideal gas law" is defined as: PV = nRT; R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); relates macroscopic state variables; valid when gas molecules are far apart and non-interacting. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Kinetic theory"?

options.text: Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
P_total = P₁ + P₂; equivalent to adding the reciprocals of focal lengths

quiz_questions.explanation: "Kinetic theory" is defined as: Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Root-mean-square speed"?

options.text: θ_incident = θ_reflected; both angles measured from the normal to the surface
f_observed = f_source · (v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source); approaching gives higher pitch, receding gives lower pitch
F_b = ρ_fluid · V_displaced · g; an object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force (average density < fluid density)
v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass

quiz_questions.explanation: "Root-mean-square speed" is defined as: v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Boltzmann constant"?

options.text: ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N
k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy
Each element has a unique set of spectral lines; used for identification from streetlights to distant stars
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself

quiz_questions.explanation: "Boltzmann constant" is defined as: k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Gas law special cases"?

options.text: 1/λ = R_H(1/n_f² - 1/n_i²); predicts hydrogen spectral line wavelengths; R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹
1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ + ...; total voltage = sum of individual voltages; same charge on each capacitor
Boyle's law (constant T): P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; Charles's law (constant P): V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂; Gay-Lussac's law (constant V): P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂
Total charge in an isolated system remains constant; charges can transfer between objects but cannot be created or destroyed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Gas law special cases" is defined as: Boyle's law (constant T): P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; Charles's law (constant P): V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂; Gay-Lussac's law (constant V): P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "PV = nRT; R = 8.314 J/(mol·K); relates macroscopic state variables; valid when gas molecules are far apart and non-interacting"?

options.text: Emission spectrum
Doppler effect
Ideal gas law
Magnetic field of a wire

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ideal gas law".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT"?

options.text: Kinetic theory
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Nuclear fusion
Wave interference

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Kinetic theory".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v_rms = √(3kT/m) = √(3RT/M); average molecular speed increases with temperature and decreases with molecular mass"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Wien's displacement law
Root-mean-square speed
Longitudinal wave

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Root-mean-square speed".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy"?

options.text: Nuclear fusion
Speed of sound
Lens power
Boltzmann constant

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Boltzmann constant".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Boyle's law (constant T): P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; Charles's law (constant P): V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂; Gay-Lussac's law (constant V): P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂"?

options.text: Second Law of Thermodynamics
Quantum tunneling
Heat engine efficiency
Gas law special cases

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Gas law special cases".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Ideal Gas Behavior is accurate?

options.text: Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
A parallel-plate capacitor has capacitance C = ε₀A/d (area A, plate separation d).
Field lines point away from positive charges and toward negative charges.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ideal Gas Behavior as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Total internal reflection occurs when light hits the boundary at an angle greater than the critical angle: θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁), and only when going from high-n to low-n medium.
The speed of sound depends on the medium's elastic and inertial properties: v = √(B/ρ) for fluids and v ≈ 331 + 0.6T m/s in air (T in °C).
At the molecular level, temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy: ½m⟨v²⟩ = (3/2)kT.
Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature: constructive interference occurs at d sin θ = mλ (bright fringes, m = 0,±1,±2,...); destructive at d sin θ = (m+½)λ (dark fringes).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ideal Gas Behavior as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Rutherford's gold foil experiment proved atoms have tiny, dense, positive nuclei.
Mass and energy are interconvertible: the mass defect in nuclear reactions is converted to kinetic energy of products and radiation.
Diverging lenses always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.
The kinetic theory of gases derives macroscopic properties from molecular motion.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ideal Gas Behavior as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Refraction and Snell's Law describes how light bends when crossing boundaries between media of different optical densities.
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
Deviations from ideal behavior occur at high pressures and low temperatures, where intermolecular forces and molecular volume become significant.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ideal Gas Behavior as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: De Broglie extended wave-particle duality: all matter has a wavelength λ = h/p.
A parallel-plate capacitor has capacitance C = ε₀A/d (area A, plate separation d).
Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Ideal Gas Behavior as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure
Electric motors: current-carrying coils in a magnetic field experience torque (F = IL × B), converting electrical energy to rotational mechanical energy
Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential
Tire pressure monitoring: as tires heat up during driving, Gay-Lussac's law predicts pressure increases at constant volume—tire pressure sensors alert drivers to dangerous changes
Lightning: charge separation in thunderclouds creates enormous electric fields (≈3 × 10⁶ V/m) that ionize air and create conductive channels, equalizing charge via massive current discharge
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Ideal Gas Behavior applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Hall effect sensors are used as position detectors in smartphones, automotive systems (crankshaft position), and brushless DC motor commutation
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)
Stellar composition: absorption lines in starlight reveal which elements are in the star's atmosphere; helium was first discovered in the Sun's spectrum before being found on Earth
Scuba diving: at depth, the increased pressure compresses air in the lungs (Boyle's law); divers must ascend slowly to avoid decompression sickness (the bends)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Ideal Gas Behavior?

options.text: Weather balloons expand as they rise because atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude, and Boyle's law predicts volume increase at lower pressure
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing
Electron diffraction microscopy: electrons with de Broglie wavelength ~0.01 nm reveal atomic-scale structures in crystals—confirming matter's wave nature
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Ideal Gas Behavior in practice?

options.text: Prescription eyeglasses: optometrists measure required correction in diopters; a -3.0 D prescription means a diverging lens with f = -0.33 m for myopia correction
Tire pressure monitoring: as tires heat up during driving, Gay-Lussac's law predicts pressure increases at constant volume—tire pressure sensors alert drivers to dangerous changes
Thermonuclear weapons use fission to trigger fusion: the fission bomb creates temperatures and pressures needed to fuse hydrogen isotopes, releasing vastly more energy
Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Ideal Gas Behavior.
